content: @dnfield  If Skia resolves cache for compiled shaders(jvanverth) on iOS, would it be possible to provide us a manual solution that would allow us to manually go through the app and after that include all the shaders in final ipa file that Skia could recompile on app start the way it is doing now on Android and iOS(where Open GL is used)?

(Side note - I was wrong, Flutter may have a little bit of work to do to make sure the cache gets saved at the right times, but it should be a relatively trivial amount of effort since we already do things like this - it will still not require any updates for app developers.)

The problem with shipping pre-built shaders is that shaders can have GPU specific features, and may not work on devices with even slightly different physical hardware compared to the hardware they were built for. However, there are "warmup" routines you can add - see https://flutter.dev/docs/perf/rendering/shader. Once persistent cache for Metal is wired up it should make it possible to do those using Metal (the docs currently say it's not possible on Metal, which is true today).

Ok, so this solution from jvanvarth is going to help with caching of shaders during runtime, but for shipping pre-build shaders we have to wait for persistent cache, do you have some deadlines for this?

Once the work in Skia is done, we can add the persistent cache changes to the engine side. I don't have an estimate of that right now, but it should be fairly trivial since we already have relevant code for it in place that just will need to be turned on.

@dnfield are you saying we need to do manual "warmup" on all the devices we'd like to support to avoid yank?

> The problem with shipping pre-built shaders is that shaders can have GPU specific features, and may not work on devices with even slightly different physical hardware compared to the hardware they were built for. However, there are "warmup" routines you can add - see https://flutter.dev/docs/perf/rendering/shader. Once persistent cache for Metal is wired up it should make it possible to do those using Metal (the docs currently say it's not possible on Metal, which is true today).

> An update from the Skia side: I started looking into adding MTLBinaryArchive support last week, and at this point it doesn't look like it will be difficult to drop it in as a side cache to our already existing persistent shader cache. I don't want to make a hard promise on completion as I have a lot of demands on my time, but I expect I should be able to get it done in the next few weeks, conservatively before the December break.

Ok, I came here from this [reddit page](https://www.reddit.com/r/FlutterDev/comments/jwmepu/very_poor_performance_of_new_google_pay_app/) about the poor performance of the new Google pay app written in Flutter . and now I'm just trying not to freak out. I have clients that have been promised great performance from Flutter. besides, I hate even thinking about going back to native android development. it's just bad and boring. and I did it for 3.5 years. And there is no other portable UI kit framework out there.

Suffice it to say, it is certainly troubling that Google itself is unable to release a performant app. Nevertheless, since we have neither the time nor the resources to completely rewrite our app, we'll continue to be cautiously optimistic about the solution to startup jank, general UI performance improvements, and the overall long-term viability of Flutter.

I still cannot understand why did they move to Metal? Yes, OpenGL is deprecated by Apple, but it was deprecated for a long time. Did not they see these awful janks? Did not the community start complaining about performance when the framework moved to Metal? I'm asking because I do not understand is there any valuable reason why we had to tolerate these changes and disadvantages of Metal?
@alectogeek This migration would be made one way or another. Obviously the process could not handled perfectly but I believe we will get a warm metal app soon.
My only complaint here is that it feels like metal was released against a deadline, not against a reasonable quality standard.

@furkansarihan Yeah, you're right. I know that it is a complicated process and I really appreciate the Flutter team's work, but let us be honest at the same time and say that it was the wrong decision to migrate the stable branch to Metal. Also, It was awful management of the project because the migration to Metal was in Spring, but we still do not have the solution. The applications written on Flutter are unusable on iOS. Our company loses (and still losing) money because of that. My reputation is damaged because it was my decision to invest in Flutter. I have no reason to be understanding now.

P.S. My offer to provide additional information about the current progress and problems on the Flutter.dev page was also rejected. (#70571) It could help to avoid the same problems as I did for new users.

Hello @arturfornal, such a sad story, but did you try flutter engine without Metal support? I solved the same problem by using a workaround  from repo, on most of iOS devices it is stable solution. Tried on iPhone 7, XS, 11 and iPad pro. https://github.com/acoutts/flutter-engines-no-metal

@Renesanse make sure you do a lot of testing across a number of devices to see if your app is truly stable. It appeared stable to us when we first tested with those OpenGL frameworks, but after our QA team repeatedly used features that had animations (especially on older 12.X devices), it became clear that the build was not as stable as it first appeared to be. We eventually saw intermittent crashes on even the newest iOS devices.

We have an enterprise-level app that we are building in flutter, and the first launch performance is horrible on iOS. This is especially bad due to onboarding -- new users will get a very bad impression, even though we use correct coding practices on our end. New users aren't going to manually warm up the animations by pressing them several times until they smooth out, they will simply go to the app store and leave a 1 star review for poor performance (which is exactly what other flutter devs reported happening to their production apps).

Furthermore, the flutter landing page falsely advertises "native performance", when due to the shader compilation issues, it is worse than react native and even web-view tools. This issue has been active for over a year now, and the flutter team inadequately addresses it with low priority, when it should be **imperative**.

We have invested a lot of time and money into this project, and we hope that this issue will be fixed by the time we release (1-2 months from now). This is not a hobby project, while many of these indie/hobby devs don't mind and pretend this is a minor issue, this is extremely important to us and our stakeholders (we have a responsibility to them and to our users as well).

I see the moderation team here loves closing similar threads that are straightforward and don't shower flutter with praise. Pretending it's tolerable and whatnot. This should not be a P3 issue, this should be P1.

Our investors are not happy with the current situation at all, and I will proceed with legal action if I have to, because I have a fiduciary duty to them in regards to their invested capital. Flutter made a misleading, deceptive statement about its performance, and if this is not fixed, it will cost my company time, money, and reputation.

Fix this issue immediately.

Totally agree, to be honest, big company where I work is making decision to use flutter or not. The situation right now is that budget xiaomi device for 50 bucks has better performance then iPhone 11.
@dnfield @Hixie I think bumping the priority on this would go a long way...
Even the iPhone 12 Pro Max has a very poor performance on first launch.

I haven't seen this strategy mentioned anywhere on GitHub, Reddit or Medium yet, so I'll post it in case it helps other companies or individuals in a similar situation.

An important thing to recognize is that, at least on the many iOS devices we've tested, across many different apps, is that the **the first-run jank is not on every animation, it's on every "unique" animation** - the threshold of what makes an animation/shader unique isn't clear, but for example, it isn't on every popup, or every screen transition, but usually only on the first instance of it.

As a result, the perceptual issue varies dramatically based on how often new "unique" shaders are introduced in the UX flow. I've found that a good temporary workaround is to reduce the variation in animations, and reuse existing ones as often as possible.

Some specific examples of what we've implemented to help with this issue:

**1. Use the same routing transition as often as possible.** 

In our experience, if you're able to use just one routing animation, the jank will only occur on the first route, even if the route contents change. Perhaps a user sees a routing jank once before signing in, and that's it. This one change alone can make a big perceptual difference, as opposed to seeing jank at minute 0, minute 3, minute 5, minute 6 and so on.

**2. Reduce the number of unique modal elements.**

It's ideal to have many different types of modals in different parts of the UI, based on context, like alerts, confirmation dialogs, tooltips, toasts, bottom sheets, and so on. But each of these will have first-run jank and it can look particularly bad. However, if you're able to temporarily consolidate those at all, and move the differences to the content itself, rather than the screen animation or container, you've reduced jank from possibly 6-7 times, spread out throughout the UX flow, to a single time, early on and more forgettable.

For example, instead of having a confirmation dialog, and an alert popup, and a tooltip, you use the same container and animation, and just change the content.

**3. Reuse the same animation effects where possible, rather than making subtly different ones based on context.**

Again, ideally you use context-specific animations and effects, but that comes at a performance cost right now. Moving to more generic animations and effects that can be used on many screens and many elements can help.

**4. Change fluid animations to be short or instant.**

Each "fluid" animation is more likely to need a shader warmup. This is hard to describe, so here are visual examples.

Animations like this will incur jank much more often:

![menu](https://cdn.dribbble.com/users/108183/screenshots/4800174/fluid_tab_bar_interaction_dribbble_shot_by_volorf_2.gif)

Animations like this won't incur jank nearly as often and often perceptibly not at all:

![menu](https://miro.medium.com/max/1410/1*_aNaM5-G9nSxAju7yYbROA.gif)

Little things like this can add up to a noticeable real-world difference while this is being resolved, and have a real impact on end-user perception of the app.

Your mileage and ability to do this will vary dramatically, of course, but I hope this helps someone.

First let me say that the whole team is also concerned about our performance on Metal, and we completely agree that it is unacceptable as is; our architecture very much assumes that shaders will be cached. We empathize deeply with anyone whose application on iOS is suffering from jank because of this issue.

We have marked this P3 because that is the appropriate priority level in Flutter's priority scheme for something that we are actively working on which is of high priority. You can read more about our prioritization scheme on the wiki (https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Issue-hygiene#priorities). In short, however, P0-P2 levels are reserved for critical issues that are even more dire than this bug, and P3 is what we use for this kind of issue.

This issue is non-trivial and work is actively happening to address it. It is our intent to have this addressed by our next stable release, but we make no promises, because that's not how engineering works.
@Hixie when is the next stable release planned?

> @Hixie Thanks for the explanation. Looking at the priority rules, it sounds like this would be a P1 issue if it was blocking someone with representation ("top-tier customer"), but if it's blocking someone without representation (small project or large project that does not have a formal relationship with Google) it's P3.
> 
> I would suggest that there should be situations where P3 can be bumped to P2 or P1 that doesn't hinge solely on their "relationship status" with Google, as a sum effect of a blocker, especially one that appears to be reputation threatening, would arguably exceed the negative impact of blocking one top-tier customer.
> 
> As a slightly humorous indicator of the seriousness of this issue, it has been reported that this is spawning conspiracy theory videos saying Apple has hamstrung Flutter intentionally by forcing migration to Metal. Maybe P1 could also be for issues that are breeding conspiracy theories. 😅

Folks, this is not a support forum, please keep comments specifically to the topic of helping with the shader compilation caching with Metal on iOS. Thanks.

May I recommend opening a separate issue to discuss posible pataches/workarounds so that this issue can stay focused on its topic?
It might be better to start a Google doc people can collaborate on. Github issues have a way of getting tough to navigate as more comments are added, and it becomes unclear who you're responding to.

TBH it's not even really clear what this issue is supposed to be tracking. Is it tracking "investigating" wiring up metal binary archives? Either way, maybe it's time to turn on GitHub discussions on this repo. I don't think a Google Doc is going to get anywhere good in a public context.
